title: Glossary and Scope of Direct Visual Inspection
narrationScript: Words are instruments in this trade, and the glossary is their calibration record. Visual testing borrows everyday words, indication, defect, evaluate, and gives them precise technical meanings. The reference for that vocabulary is the glossary chapter of the Nondestructive Testing Handbook volume on visual testing. When your report uses those words the way the handbook defines them, an engineer three time zones away can act on your findings without a phone call. When it does not, your report creates work and risk. So let's calibrate. Begin with the chain every finding moves through. You see something. The moment you notice it, it is an indication. Nothing more. An indication is simply a response that needs explaining. Interpretation is the step where you decide what the indication is. Some indications are false. They come from glare, a smudge, a water stain, something other than a real condition of the part. Clean the surface, fix the light, look again, and they vanish. Some indications are nonrelevant. They are real, but they come from the part's design or an acceptable process condition, like a machining step or a weld ripple pattern, and they have no bearing on acceptance. What remains is a relevant indication, one caused by an actual discontinuity. A discontinuity is any interruption in the normal structure of the part. Notice that the word carries no judgment. A discontinuity is not good or bad yet. Evaluation is the judgment step. You compare the relevant indication against the acceptance criteria in the governing document. If it passes, it remains a discontinuity and the part is acceptable. Only if it fails the criteria does it earn the word defect. A defect, by definition, is rejectable. Use that word early and you have rejected the part in writing, whether you meant to or not. Now the seeing words. Illuminance is the light arriving at a surface. We measure it in lux or footcandles, where one footcandle is about ten point seven six lux, and we verify it with a calibrated meter held at the surface. Luminance is the light leaving a surface toward your eye, and the difference in luminance between a flaw and its background is contrast, which is what actually makes detection possible. Resolution is the ability of an optical system to separate fine detail. Visual acuity is your own resolving power, checked with a near vision card at least annually under your employer's written practice. Accommodation is your eye changing focus for near objects, and it slows as we age. Adaptation is your eye adjusting to a new light level, and full dark adaptation takes about thirty minutes. Direct visual testing means your eye gets within six hundred millimeters, that is twenty four inches, of the surface, at an angle of at least thirty degrees, with mirrors and magnifiers allowed as helpers. Remote visual testing puts an instrument, a borescope, a fiberscope, a video probe, between your eye and the surface, and it must resolve detail at least as well as your eye would directly. Translucent visual testing uses light transmitted through a material to judge its interior. Last, scope. The CP one zero five outline covers the whole visual testing method, including all the remote and enhanced technology. But the outline itself recognizes that many employers only need direct visual inspection. In that case, the employer's written practice may delete the topics that exist only for remote or enhanced visual work. Borescope and fiberscope construction. Video probe systems. Automated and machine vision. Translucent techniques. What can never be deleted is the core. Vision and lighting. Viewing geometry. Discontinuities and where they come from. Acceptance criteria and the codes that hold them. Records and reports. And here is the contract that comes with the trimming. Your certification is then limited to direct visual inspection. The limitation is documented in the written practice and reflected in your certification record. The day a job needs a borescope is the day you need additional training and certification, not improvisation. Scope limits are honest. Working outside them is not. Carry the vocabulary precisely, know exactly what your certification covers, and your reports will stand up to any reader, any auditor, and any amount of time.

sections.heading: The Glossary Is a Working Tool
sections.body:
Level II reports get read by people who never saw the part: engineers, auditors, customers, sometimes lawyers. The vocabulary that makes those reports portable is standardized in the glossary of the ASNT *Nondestructive Testing Handbook*, Vol. 9: *Visual Testing*, third edition — Chapter 13 — and CP-105 points there as the reference vocabulary for this method. The definitions in this lesson are this course's own plain-language restatements of how those terms are used in practice; when a dispute gets formal, the handbook glossary is the arbiter.

The heart of the vocabulary is a chain of escalating words: **indication** (anything you notice that needs explaining), **interpretation** (deciding whether it is false, nonrelevant, or relevant), **discontinuity** (a real interruption in the part's normal structure — a neutral word with no judgment attached), **evaluation** (comparing a relevant indication to the acceptance criteria), and **defect** (only what evaluation rejects). Each word commits you to exactly one step of that chain. Write 'defect' in a report when you mean 'indication' and you have rejected hardware in writing; write 'indication' after evaluation is complete and you have hidden a decision your reader needs. Precision here is not pedantry — it is the difference between a report and a liability.

Most employer procedures carry a definitions section for exactly this reason. When your procedure defines a term, that definition governs your report; when it is silent, the handbook glossary is the default reference. Either way, the one unacceptable option is a private definition of your own — vocabulary is controlled exactly the way calibration is.

sections.caption: Every finding moves through the same chain — indication, interpretation, evaluation — and only a rejectable result earns the word defect.
Core VT working vocabulary, restated in original course wording from the usage standardized in the handbook glossary.
rows: Indication
Any observed response or condition that requires explanation before the part can be judged
False indication
An indication caused by something other than a real part condition — glare, a smudge, a stain; it disappears when lighting or cleanliness is corrected
Nonrelevant indication
A real but acceptable condition arising from design or process, such as a machining step or weld ripple, with no bearing on acceptance
Relevant indication
An indication caused by an actual discontinuity; it proceeds to evaluation
Discontinuity
Any interruption in the normal physical structure of a part — a neutral term, not inherently rejectable
Defect
A discontinuity whose type, size, or location fails the acceptance criteria — rejectable by definition
Interpretation
Deciding what an indication is: false, nonrelevant, or relevant
Evaluation
Comparing a relevant indication against the acceptance criteria to accept or reject
Acceptance criteria
The documented limits in the governing code, standard, or specification against which evaluation is performed
Direct VT
Examination with the eye within 600 mm (24 in.) of the surface at not less than 30°, with mirrors and magnifiers permitted as aids
Remote VT
Examination through an instrument — borescope, fiberscope, video probe — with resolution at least equivalent to direct viewing
Translucent VT
Technique using transmitted light to assess the interior of translucent materials such as laminates

sections.heading: Light and Optics Terms You Must Use Correctly
sections.body: **Illuminance** is light *arriving at* a surface, measured in lux or footcandles (1 fc ≈ 10.76 lux); procedure minimums — commonly about 500 lux general and 1000 lux for critical examination — are illuminance values, verified with a calibrated meter at the surface. **Luminance** is light *leaving* a surface toward the eye; the luminance difference between a flaw and its background is **contrast**, and contrast — not raw brightness — is what makes detection possible. **Resolution** is an optical system's ability to separate fine detail; **visual acuity** is the examiner's own resolving power, checked by near-vision acuity examination (for example Jaeger J-1 or J-2 at a specified distance) at least annually per the written practice — 20/20 vision corresponds to resolving roughly one arc-minute. **Accommodation** is the eye refocusing for near distance; it degrades with age, which is exactly why reading-distance acuity checks matter. **Adaptation** is adjustment to a new ambient light level; full dark adaptation takes about 30 minutes. **Specular reflection** is mirror-like — glare lives here — while **diffuse reflection** scatters light and is kinder to inspection. Misusing these terms on a report, like quoting a lux value measured 'at the lamp' or calling your acuity 'resolution,' signals exactly the kind of imprecision auditors go hunting for.

sections.heading: When the Scope Is Direct VT Only
sections.body:
The CP-105 VT outline covers the full method — direct, remote, translucent, automated. But the outline itself notes that when an employer's work requires **only direct visual inspection**, the written practice may *delete the topics that exist solely for the other techniques*: borescope and fiberscope optics and construction, video probe (CCD/CMOS) systems, automated and machine-vision systems, and translucent techniques. What may never be trimmed is the direct-VT core: vision and lighting fundamentals, viewing geometry (600 mm / 30°), simple aids such as mirrors and magnifiers, discontinuity origins and recognition, codes and acceptance criteria, and records.

The trimming carries obligations. The deletion must be documented in the employer's written practice; training hours and examinations then match the reduced outline; and the resulting **certification is limited in scope** — the certification record should make the direct-VT-only limitation visible. Practically: a direct-VT-only Level II handed a borescope job must stop, because operating outside certified scope invalidates the examination and exposes the employer and the examiner alike. Scope limitation is a legitimate, efficient tool for focused programs — pretending the limitation away is a program violation, and an auditor will read your certification scope before reading anything else.

sections.caption: When certification is limited to direct visual inspection, the remote and enhanced-technology topics may be deleted from the outline, but the direct-VT core always remains.

sections.title: Say the word you can defend
sections.body: Until evaluation is complete, it is an **indication**. Once interpretation says it is real and caused by an actual condition, it is a **discontinuity**. Only when it fails the written acceptance criteria does it become a **defect**. Reports that use these three words precisely survive audits; reports that use them loosely become exhibits.

figures.title: From Indication to Defect: The Vocabulary Chain
Direct-VT-Only Certification: What Stays, What May Drop

interactive.title: Direct-VT-Only Scope: Keep or Delete?
interactive.intro: Your employer's written practice limits VT certification to direct visual inspection only. Classify each training topic: must it stay in the outline, or may it be deleted under CP-105's direct-VT-only provision?
buckets: May be deleted for direct-VT-only scope

items.label: Vision, lighting levels, and viewing geometry (600 mm / 30°)
items.why: Direct VT is built on the examiner's eye, verified lighting, and viewing geometry — this is the core of the method and can never be trimmed.

items.label: Borescope and fiberscope optics and construction
items.why: Rigid and fiber-optic scopes exist only for remote VT; with no remote work in scope, the topic may be deleted from the outline.

items.label: Video probe systems and CCD/CMOS imaging
items.why: Video borescopes are remote VT instruments, so the topic falls outside a direct-only scope.

items.label: Discontinuities by product form (castings, welds, service damage)
items.why: Recognizing what flaws look like and where they originate is required no matter how the surface is viewed.

items.label: Translucent laminate examination technique
items.why: Translucent VT is a distinct transmitted-light technique beyond direct surface viewing, so it may be deleted from a direct-only outline.

items.label: Codes, standards, and acceptance criteria
items.why: Every evaluation — direct or otherwise — is made against documented acceptance criteria; this topic can never be deleted.

items.label: Automated and machine-vision inspection systems
items.why: Automated vision systems are enhanced-technology topics that apply only beyond direct viewing, so they are deletable for a direct-only scope.

items.label: Recording and reporting examination results
items.why: Documentation and traceability requirements apply to every examination regardless of technique, so the topic always stays.

checkQuestions.question: A surface condition has been evaluated against the acceptance criteria and does not meet them. The correct term for it is now:
options: An indication
A discontinuity
A defect
An artifact
checkQuestions.explanation: A defect is, by definition, a discontinuity that fails the acceptance criteria — the word only applies after evaluation rejects the condition.

checkQuestions.question: An indication turns out to be caused by glare from the lamp rather than by any actual condition of the part. It is classified as:
options: A relevant indication
A false indication
A nonrelevant indication
A defect
checkQuestions.explanation: False indications come from something other than a real part condition — glare, smudges, stains — and disappear when lighting or cleanliness is corrected.

checkQuestions.question: Illuminance, measured in lux or footcandles, describes:
options: Light arriving at the examination surface
Light leaving the surface toward the eye
The examiner's near-vision acuity
The contrast ratio of a flaw to its background
checkQuestions.explanation: Illuminance is the light falling on the surface, which is why the meter sensor is placed at the surface; light leaving the surface toward the eye is luminance.

checkQuestions.question: Direct visual testing is generally defined as examination with:
options: Any optical instrument of at least 10× magnification
The eye within 600 mm (24 in.) of the surface at not less than 30°, with mirrors and magnifiers permitted as aids
A video probe whose monitor sits within 600 mm of the examiner
Transmitted light passing through the part
checkQuestions.explanation: Direct VT means the examiner's eye has adequate access — within 600 mm at 30° or better — and simple aids such as mirrors and magnifiers do not change that classification.

checkQuestions.question: Under CP-105, when an employer's written practice limits VT certification to direct visual inspection only:
options: All outline topics must still be trained in full
Topics that apply only to remote or enhanced techniques — borescopes, video systems, translucent VT, machine vision — may be deleted, and the certification scope is limited accordingly
The lighting and viewing-geometry topics may be deleted first
The employer no longer needs a written practice
checkQuestions.explanation: The outline allows deletion of remote- and enhanced-technique topics for a direct-only scope, but the limitation must be documented and the certification is then limited to direct VT.

references: CP-105-2024 VT Topical Outline, Level II §11.1–11.2
ASNT Nondestructive Testing Handbook, Vol. 9: Visual Testing, third edition, Chapter 13 glossary (terminology reference — definitions restated in original course wording)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 9 direct and remote VT practice (generic reference)
SNT-TC-1A written practice and certification scope provisions (generic reference)
